app.name: v0
app.bestFor: Production React/Next.js components, deploy straight to Vercel. Developer default.
app.rankBasis: The only app placed near the top across all three cited 2026 UI-generation roundups (EPAM, Muzli, and Komposo), reinforced by its first-party Vercel integration and dominant visibility in React/shadcn workflows.
app.pricingFree: Free plan: $5 of included monthly credits
app.pricingPaid: Plus $30/user/month — $30 of included monthly credits, higher rate limits, and private projects
features: Image or Figma input to editable code
pros: reviewers consistently rate its React/shadcn output among the most production-ready
generated code stays visible, editable, and easy to move into an existing repository
very short path from prompt to a deployed Vercel preview
cons: strong Next.js and Vercel bias makes it less neutral for other stacks
repeated visual iterations can consume credits quickly
less complete than full-stack builders for backend-heavy applications

app.name: Lovable
app.bestFor: Turn a prompt into a full working MVP app. Best for non-developers.
app.rankBasis: Featured as a leading prompt-to-app choice in all three cited comparisons (Banani, EPAM, and Muzli), with reviewers repeatedly singling it out as the easiest route from an idea to a working MVP.
app.pricingFree: Free plan: daily build-credit grants with public projects; grants expire each day
app.pricingPaid: Pro $25/month — 100 monthly credits, daily build-credit grants, custom domains, and user roles
pros: non-developers can reach a functional, polished MVP unusually quickly
integrated backend, authentication, and deployment reduce setup work
conversational iteration is approachable for product and design users
cons: credit use becomes noticeable during debugging and repeated revisions
users report that larger generated codebases become harder to steer reliably
complex fixes can trigger iteration loops that still require developer intervention

app.name: Bolt.new
app.bestFor: Full-stack apps (frontend + backend + DB) generated in-browser. Full prototypes.
app.rankBasis: A top-tier pick in both cited hands-on 2026 comparisons (EPAM and Komposo), distinguished from UI-only generators by building and running a full-stack project directly in its browser environment.
app.pricingFree: Free plan: 300,000-token daily limit and 1M tokens/month
app.pricingPaid: Pro $25/month — starts at 10M tokens/month, no daily token limit, and unused tokens roll over for one additional month
pros: reviewers value seeing and running the complete application without local setup
handles frontend, backend, and database scaffolding in one workflow
fast live previews make early prototype iteration highly tangible
cons: token usage rises sharply as project files and context grow
community reports describe regressions or repeated fixes on longer sessions
browser execution is less flexible than a fully controlled local development environment

app.name: Figma Make
app.bestFor: Highest design fidelity if you already live in Figma (reads real frame metadata).
app.rankBasis: Ranks prominently in both cited 2026 comparisons, and uniquely benefits from Figma's established design-system workflow by bringing library styling context and editable design layers into prompt-to-code generation.
app.pricingPaid: Professional Full seat $16/month billed annually — 3,000 AI credits/month and full access to Figma Make, Design, Dev Mode, Sites, and FigJam
pros: strongest workflow continuity for teams already designing in Figma
can use real design-system context instead of approximating a brand from text
generated prototypes remain easy to share and critique with design stakeholders
cons: serious usage is tied to a paid Full seat and finite AI credits
reviewers find generated code less maintainable than hand-structured production code
backend and deployment workflows are less complete than dedicated full-stack builders

app.name: Replit Agent
app.bestFor: Build and host an app end-to-end. Most beginner-friendly.
app.rankBasis: Included in EPAM's tested five-tool comparison and backed by Replit's much broader, mature browser-IDE community, but it has fewer UI-design-specific roundup placements than the four products above it.
app.pricingFree: Starter: free with daily Agent credit grants
app.pricingPaid: Replit Core $20/month billed annually ($25 monthly) — $25 of monthly usage credits and access to the full Agent
pros: beginners get coding, runtime, hosting, and deployment in one workspace
the agent can inspect and modify a real running project rather than only output snippets
collaboration and remixing benefit from Replit's established developer community
cons: usage-based Agent and hosting charges can be difficult to predict
users report agent loops and incomplete fixes on complex projects
applications can become dependent on Replit-specific deployment and infrastructure

skill.name: shadcn/ui
skill.bestFor: The dominant modern UI approach (~119.7k★) — copy-paste, own-your-code React components (Radix + Tailwind) that coding agents build most component UIs from.
skill.rankBasis: The dominant own-your-code React UI project at roughly 120k GitHub stars, with unusually broad adoption in AI-generated interfaces and a component registry used directly by major coding tools.
features: Tailwind styling with full source ownership
CLI and registry for component distribution
pros: developers own and can freely modify every component instead of depending on a runtime package
excellent compatibility with coding agents because its patterns are widely represented
accessible primitives and polished defaults accelerate production UI work
cons: copied components do not receive centralized package upgrades automatically
teams still have to assemble and govern their own coherent component system
heavy default adoption can make products look visually similar without deliberate customization

skill.name: Tailwind CSS
skill.bestFor: The utility-first CSS framework (~96k★) underpinning shadcn/ui and daisyUI — the de-facto styling layer for modern component work.
skill.rankBasis: One of the most adopted styling projects in the category at roughly 96k GitHub stars and hundreds of millions of recent npm downloads, underpinning shadcn/ui, daisyUI, and a large share of agent-generated web UIs.
pros: reviewers consistently value the speed of styling without switching between component and CSS files
shared utility constraints make spacing and visual tokens more consistent
large ecosystem and strong agent familiarity make examples and integrations easy to find
cons: long utility strings can make markup dense and difficult to scan
the class vocabulary has an upfront learning curve
dynamically constructed class names require care to be detected by the build process

skill.name: Frontend Design (Anthropic official)
skill.bestFor: Anthropic's official, first-party Claude Code skill for distinctive, intentional UI — deliberate palette, typography and layout over templated defaults; auto-invoked for frontend work.
skill.rankBasis: The first-party frontend-design skill shipped in Anthropic's roughly 50k-star Claude Code repository, giving it stronger provenance and distribution than third-party prompt packs despite limited standalone review data.
pros: official maintenance keeps the guidance aligned with Claude Code behavior
directly targets common generic AI-interface patterns
works across frameworks because it guides design decisions rather than prescribing a component library
cons: primarily useful inside Claude Code rather than as a universal package
provides design direction, not reusable components or a tested design system
visual quality remains model- and prompt-dependent, with little independent benchmark evidence

skill.name: UI/UX Pro Max
skill.bestFor: Massively popular all-in-one design-intelligence skill (~109.6k★) — 50+ styles, 161 palettes, 57 font pairings and UX guidelines across 10 stacks (note: an 8-month-old repo whose star surge outpaces its real-world track record).
skill.rankBasis: Its roughly 110k GitHub stars make it the largest dedicated UI-design skill by raw adoption signal, but the repository is young and its exceptionally rapid star growth provides less proven usage history than the projects ranked above it.
features: Searchable UI style and pattern library
pros: broad reference coverage gives agents concrete options instead of vague aesthetic instructions
supports many popular frontend stacks and agent environments
bundles visual direction and UX checks into one installable skill
cons: independent usage evidence is thin relative to its unusually fast star growth
large prescriptive reference sets can produce formulaic output if applied without judgment
quality varies by stack and still depends on the host agent following the guidance correctly

skill.name: Material UI (MUI)
skill.bestFor: The longest-standing React component library (~98.6k★) implementing Google's Material Design — the enterprise default for years.
skill.rankBasis: A long-standing enterprise React default with roughly 99k GitHub stars, very high package adoption, and years of Stack Overflow and community coverage that substantially exceed newer component libraries.
pros: one of the broadest and best-documented React component catalogs
mature accessibility, theming, and enterprise support patterns
large user base makes troubleshooting and hiring familiarity easier
cons: the default Material visual language takes significant work to make distinctive
styling and component APIs can feel complex for deeply customized designs
major-version migrations and bundle weight are recurring community concerns

skill.name: Ant Design
skill.bestFor: Enterprise-class React component library (~98.8k★) — comprehensive components, hugely adopted in enterprise and across Asia.
skill.rankBasis: Roughly 99k GitHub stars and deep enterprise adoption make Ant Design one of the category's two most established full React suites, with especially strong usage in data-heavy products and Asian engineering communities.
features: Advanced forms, tables, and data display
pros: exceptionally complete controls for admin, analytics, and internal-tool interfaces
consistent interaction patterns reduce design work on large enterprise products
strong localization and international community support
cons: the highly opinionated visual system can be difficult to rebrand deeply
full-suite usage can add substantial bundle and CSS weight
community reports often cite complexity around advanced forms, tables, and customization

skill.name: daisyUI
skill.bestFor: The most popular Tailwind component library (~41.8k★) — semantic class names and themes layered on top of Tailwind.
skill.rankBasis: The leading semantic component layer for Tailwind at roughly 42k GitHub stars, with multi-million monthly npm usage and far broader adoption than most Tailwind component plugins.
pros: dramatically shortens markup compared with composing every component from raw utilities
large built-in theme set makes prototyping and dark mode quick
framework-agnostic CSS approach adds no JavaScript runtime
cons: semantic abstractions reduce fine-grained control compared with plain Tailwind
default components and themes can look generic without customization
teams inherit both daisyUI conventions and the underlying Tailwind dependency

skill.name: Chakra UI
skill.bestFor: Accessible, composable React component system (~40.5k★) — a popular choice for SaaS product UIs.
skill.rankBasis: A mature accessible React system with roughly 41k GitHub stars and years of SaaS adoption, though its current ecosystem momentum and component breadth trail MUI and Ant Design.
pros: developers praise its approachable component APIs and fast layout composition
accessible defaults remove substantial keyboard and ARIA work
theme tokens work well for custom SaaS design systems
cons: the v3 architecture and migration introduced meaningful upgrade churn
component breadth is smaller than the largest enterprise suites
runtime styling and generated CSS can complicate performance and debugging

skill.name: Radix UI (Primitives)
skill.bestFor: Low-level accessible, unstyled React primitives (~19.1k★) — the foundation shadcn/ui is built on.
skill.rankBasis: Roughly 19k GitHub stars understate its influence: Radix is the interaction and accessibility foundation beneath shadcn/ui and many modern React design systems.
pros: handles difficult accessibility and interaction behavior without imposing a visual style
composable primitives fit highly custom design systems
battle-tested indirectly through shadcn/ui and many downstream libraries
cons: teams must supply all visual styling and higher-level component conventions
low-level composition requires more code than a finished component suite
portals, focus behavior, and state-based animations have a learning curve

skill.name: Awesome Design Skills (bergside)
skill.bestFor: Curated, organically-adopted list (~2k★) of 67 DESIGN.md / SKILL.md files for agentic design work — a directory rather than a single installable skill.
skill.rankBasis: A roughly 2k-star curated directory covering 67 agent design skills, a credible organic discovery signal but much smaller adoption than the installable skills and libraries ranked above it.
features: Coverage across visual and UX disciplines
pros: saves time discovering specialized design prompts and workflows
shows multiple approaches instead of locking users into one design philosophy
useful reference material for authoring or evaluating custom design skills
cons: it is a directory rather than one directly installable, maintained skill
linked resources vary in quality, scope, and update cadence
adoption and independent validation are modest compared with the leading repositories

notes: Skill/Repo track rebuilt to rank by real adoption, not form factor. The recognized OSS UI leaders (shadcn/ui, Tailwind, MUI, Ant Design, and the primitives behind them) rank as kind:repo alongside the standout design skills: Anthropic's official first-party frontend-design skill and the very popular UI/UX Pro Max. Note: UI/UX Pro Max (~109.6k★) is real but its star surge on a young repo outpaces its actual track record, so it ranks below shadcn/Tailwind/the official skill rather than at the very top. Two other heavily-starred DESIGN.md collections (VoltAgent/awesome-design-md ~104k) show the same inflation pattern and were left out in favor of the more organic bergside list. Removed all MCP entries (shadcn MCP, Figma Dev Mode MCP, AIDesigner MCP). Star counts verified live 2026-07-24.
